era_context: The late 240s CE were a period of relative stability under Philip the Arab, though underlying economic and military pressures were building. The Sasanid Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power. In China, the Three Kingdoms period continued with Wei dominance. Christianity was growing but still faced sporadic persecution.

events.title: Philip the Arab consolidates power; celebrates his reign with games and spectacles
events.description: Philip the Arab, who had become emperor in 244 CE after the assassination of Gordian III, consolidated his power in 248 CE through a combination of military appointments, diplomatic marriages, and public spectacles. He distributed donatives to the troops and organized games in Rome to celebrate his reign and build popular support. Despite these efforts, Philip faced mounting pressures from plague, economic inflation, and barbarian threats on the frontiers. His reign, though relatively stable, was ultimately unsustainable.

sources.name: Historia Augusta, Life of Philip the Arab

events.certainty_note: Philip's consolidation of power is attested in sources, but specific events in 248 CE are not always clearly distinguished from those of 247 or 249.

events.title: Sasanid Persia under Shapur I continues expansion and consolidation
events.description: Shapur I, who had become Sasanid King of Kings around 240 CE, continued to consolidate power and expand Sasanid influence in the Near East. In 248 CE, Shapur was strengthening the Sasanid administrative system and military, preparing for the aggressive campaigns against Rome that would begin in earnest in the 250s. Shapur's reign marked a high point of Sasanid power and cultural achievement.

sources.name: Shapur I's Res Gestae Divi Sapuris (trilingual inscription)

events.certainty_note: Shapur's reign and consolidation of power are well-attested, but specific events in 248 CE are not clearly documented.

events.title: Wei Dynasty continues dominance in northern China under Cao Rui
events.description: Cao Rui, the fourth emperor of the Wei Dynasty, continued his reign in 248 CE. Wei remained the dominant power in northern China, though the state faced internal challenges from court factionalism and the growing power of military generals like Sima Yi. Cao Rui's reign was marked by cultural achievements, including the patronage of literature and the arts, but also by increasing political instability.
key_figures: Sima Yi (military general, growing in power)

sources.name: Records of Wei (Wei Zhi), part of the Sanguozhi

events.certainty_note: Cao Rui's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Christian communities face sporadic local persecutions; theological debates intensify
events.region: Roman Empire (widespread, especially North Africa and Asia Minor)
events.description: In 248 CE, Christian communities across the Roman Empire faced sporadic local persecutions, though no empire-wide systematic persecution had yet been initiated. Theological debates within Christianity were intensifying, particularly regarding the nature of Christ, the authority of bishops, and the proper response to persecution. These debates would shape Christian theology for centuries to come. The growth of Christianity was beginning to attract the attention of pagan intellectuals and the imperial authorities.
key_figures: Cyprian of Carthage (Christian bishop and theologian)

sources.name: Cyprian of Carthage, Letters and treatises

events.certainty_note: Christian theological debates and sporadic persecutions are attested in contemporary sources, though specific events in 248 CE are not always clearly distinguished from those of adjacent years.

disconfirming_evidence: The Historia Augusta's account of Philip the Arab is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The extent of Philip's consolidation of power and the success of his policies are debated; some scholars argue that his reign was less stable than traditional accounts suggest. The dating of specific events in Shapur I's reign is uncertain, and the chronology of his campaigns is debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 248 CE is moderate but uneven. Roman sources are relatively abundant but often written decades later. Chinese sources are contemporary or near-contemporary and generally reliable. Sources for other regions are sparse. The Historia Augusta is a major source for this period but is notoriously unreliable. Modern scholarship increasingly relies on numismatic evidence and archaeological data to supplement textual accounts.

graph_edges.note: Philip's consolidation efforts in 248 ultimately failed to prevent his overthrow in 249
Sporadic persecutions in 248 foreshadowed Decius's systematic persecution in 250
